name: Dyer Mine
short_description: BLM-managed rockhounding site near Vernal with colorful agate and jasper specimens. Popular for beginner and intermediate collectors seeking quality desert gemstones.
description:
Dyer Mine is a productive rockhounding location on Utah BLM land in Uintah County, approximately 25 miles south of Vernal. The site is known for yielding fine specimens of agate, jasper, and chalcedony in shades of red, brown, cream, and translucent white. The mine workings and surrounding exposures provide excellent access to mineralized zones without requiring extensive hiking or scrambling.

The area sits within the Uinta Basin, a structurally complex region where Tertiary volcanic rocks and sedimentary deposits have been chemically altered and enriched with silica-rich groundwater. Agate formation occurred as silica precipitated along fractures and within host rock over millions of years, creating the banded and solid specimens found here today. Historical prospecting and small-scale mining in this region dates back over a century, with collectors continuing to find quality material in exposed banks and wash gravels.

Collectors should focus on the old mine dump areas and weathered exposures where specimens weather out naturally. The terrain is open, high desert with sparse vegetation, making it easy to spot translucent and brightly colored stones. This is an ideal location for families and newer rockhounds, as material is accessible without specialized equipment or extensive backcountry travel.

Due to BLM regulations, personal use collection is permitted with a 25-pound daily limit. Bring water, sun protection, and sturdy shoes for walking across rocky terrain. Late spring and fall visits avoid the intense summer heat while offering excellent collecting conditions.
history: The Uinta Basin experienced significant Tertiary volcanism and hydrothermal activity that deposited silica-rich fluids throughout the region, creating ideal conditions for agate and jasper formation. Historical mining and prospecting in this area targeted various minerals, with Dyer Mine becoming a well-known spot for collectors seeking quality gemstone specimens.
tips: Focus your search on the exposed banks and old mine tailings where weathered specimens are most visible against the lighter-colored host rock. Use a hand lens to identify translucent zones within agate nodules - these clear to milky areas often mark the highest-quality material. Bring a small chisel and hammer for carefully extracting specimens from the surrounding matrix, and check the ground after rainfall when wet surfaces make colors more visible. Work the wash gravels and decomposed material around the mine area where naturally freed specimens collect.
terrain_notes: Open high desert terrain at approximately 5,800 feet elevation with scattered low sagebrush and sparse piñon cover. Walking surface is rocky, uneven ground with loose material and embedded stones. The old mine site includes excavated banks and scattered dumps providing excellent specimen visibility. Parking to collecting area is generally a short walk of under one-quarter mile across relatively flat to gently rolling ground.
best_season: April - June and September - November. Late spring and early fall offer comfortable temperatures and lower UV intensity. Summer heat regularly exceeds 90 degrees Fahrenheit in this region, while winter snow and cold can make access difficult.
written_directions: From Vernal, take US 191 south for approximately 18 miles toward the town of Myton. Turn east on local county road and follow signs toward the Dyer Mine area or historic mining sites. Continue for approximately 7 miles on improved dirt road to the main site parking area. High-clearance vehicles are recommended for the final access road, especially in wet conditions.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel roads in good condition during dry weather. May become rough or impassable after heavy rain or snowmelt. Final approach road benefits from high-clearance vehicles but is passable for standard passenger cars under normal conditions.
parking_notes: Informal parking area at or near the old mine site with space for several vehicles. No developed facilities or parking fee. Parking is on BLM land with no formal lot structure.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. All collected material must remain within personal use limits. Specimens must be removed by hand tools only.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
No large-scale mining or digging
nearest_services: Vernal, Utah - 25 miles north. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and grocery stores. Myton - approximately 12 miles west with limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat, open terrain with short walking distances from parking. Uneven rocky ground with loose material requires sturdy footwear but no scrambling or climbing. Suitable for most fitness levels and families. No wheelchair access to the actual collecting areas due to rocky terrain.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at Dyer Mine near Vernal, Utah?
faq.answer: Dyer Mine is known for excellent agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. You'll find material in shades of red, brown, cream, and translucent white. Agate nodules are particularly common, with some specimens showing beautiful banding and translucent zones. The site is accessible year-round, though spring and fall offer the best collecting conditions.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Dyer Mine on BLM land in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting at Dyer Mine. As BLM public land in Utah, it is open to personal use collection under standard FLPMA regulations. You are limited to 25 pounds per day and must collect by hand tools only. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are prohibited.

faq.question: How far is Dyer Mine from Vernal and what are the driving conditions?
faq.answer: Dyer Mine is approximately 25 miles south of Vernal, Utah. Take US 191 south toward Myton, then follow county roads east for about 7 miles to reach the site. The final access road is improved dirt and gravel, passable for most passenger vehicles in dry conditions, though high-clearance is recommended. Roads may be rough after rain or snow.

faq.question: Is Dyer Mine rockhounding site suitable for families and beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Dyer Mine is excellent for families and beginners. The terrain is open, relatively flat high desert with short walking distances from parking. Specimens are visible and accessible without specialized equipment or technical rock scrambling. Children ages 6 and up can participate with adult supervision. The site attracts collectors of all skill levels.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect agate and jasper at Dyer Mine?
faq.answer: Basic hand tools are all you need: a small chisel, rock hammer, and collecting bag. A 10x hand lens helps identify translucent zones in agate. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, sturdy hiking boots, and a hat - the open desert offers no shade. A small daypack makes carrying tools and specimens convenient.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Dyer Mine in Utah?
faq.answer: April through June and September through November are ideal. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures and lower UV intensity. Summer heat regularly exceeds 90 degrees Fahrenheit with minimal shade, and winter can bring snow and cold. Late spring and early fall provide the most pleasant collecting experience and best specimen visibility.

meta_title: Dyer Mine Rockhounding - Find Agate & Jasper in Utah
meta_description: Rockhound for agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Dyer Mine near Vernal, Utah. BLM public land, no permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Perfect for families.
